verses.commentary: Everyone who believes Jesus is the Christ is born of God. Faith in Christ as Messiah proves regeneration. Believing is not intellectual assent but trusting allegiance. Everyone who loves the Father loves His child as well. Family love is natural. Loving God means loving His children. Vertical and horizontal love are inseparable. Genuine faith produces love for God's family.

This is how we know we love God's children: by loving God and carrying out His commands. The order is surprising. We might expect loving God proves we love others. But John says loving God and obeying proves love for believers. Love for God's people is tested by obedience to God. True love is not sentimentality but submission to God's will. Obedience validates love.

This is love for God: to keep His commands. Love equals obedience. His commands are not burdensome. Obedience is not oppressive to the regenerate. The new heart delights in God's law (Psalm 1:2, 119:97). What was once a burden is now a joy. The yoke is easy, the burden light (Matthew 11:30). Love makes obedience natural, not grievous. Grace transforms duty into delight.

Everyone born of God overcomes the world. New birth produces victory. The world is the system opposed to God. This is the victory: our faith. Faith is the means of overcoming. Faith in Christ defeats the world's power, lies, and temptations. The victory is not future but present. Believers overcome now through trusting Christ. Faith is the victory.

Who overcomes the world? Only the one who believes Jesus is the Son of God. Victory is exclusive to faith in Christ. Belief in Jesus' deity and messiahship is essential. No other faith overcomes. No human effort suffices. Only trust in Jesus, the Son of God, defeats the world. Christianity is not one option among many; it is the only way. Faith in Christ is the singular means of victory.

Jesus Christ came by water and blood. Water likely refers to His baptism (identifying with sinners, beginning His ministry). Blood refers to His death (atonement). Not by water only, but by water and blood. Some Gnostics accepted Jesus' baptism but denied His death's salvific significance. John insists both are essential. The Spirit testifies because the Spirit is truth. The Spirit affirms Christ's identity and work. Baptism, crucifixion, and Spirit's witness together confirm Jesus is the Christ.

Three that testify. This introduces the threefold witness. In Jewish law, two or three witnesses establish truth (Deuteronomy 19:15). God provides abundant testimony to His Son. The verse in KJV (Johannine Comma) referencing Father, Word, Spirit is not in early manuscripts and is likely a later addition. The original refers to the three in verse 8.

The Spirit, the water, and the blood, and the three are in agreement. Spirit: internal testimony to believers. Water: Christ's baptism. Blood: Christ's death. Three witnesses, one message. God provides multiple, converging testimonies to Jesus' identity and work. The Spirit testifies internally; the historical events (baptism and crucifixion) testify externally. All point to Christ as Savior.

We accept human testimony, but God's testimony is greater. Human courts require two or three witnesses. God provides testimony about His Son. If we trust human witnesses, how much more should we trust God's witness? His testimony is superior in authority and reliability. God has spoken; we must listen.

Whoever believes in the Son of God accepts this testimony. Belief in Christ means accepting God's witness about Him. Whoever does not believe makes God out to be a liar. Unbelief is not neutral; it accuses God of falsehood. Rejecting Jesus is rejecting God's testimony. Unbelief is blasphemy. To deny Christ is to call God a liar. The stakes are ultimate.

This is the testimony: God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son. The core message. Eternal life is God's gift, located in Christ. Life is not abstract but personal: in the Son. Salvation is inseparable from Jesus. You cannot have the life without the Person. This is the gospel summary.

Whoever has the Son has life. Whoever does not have the Son does not have life. Binary. No middle ground. Possessing Christ is possessing life. Lacking Christ is lacking life. Eternal destiny hinges on relationship with Jesus. This is exclusive, uncompromising, clear. Christianity's scandal: salvation is only in Christ. But it is also Christianity's glory: all who have Christ have life. Assurance or warning, depending on relationship to Jesus.

I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know you have eternal life. Purpose of the letter: assurance. John writes to believers, not skeptics. Knowing is possible. Assurance is not arrogance but faith resting on God's promise. Believing in the name means trusting in all that Jesus is and has done. The name represents His person and work. Those who believe can know they have eternal life now. Present possession, not future hope. Certainty is the gift of God to believers.

Confidence in approaching God: if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us. Prayer is effective when aligned with God's will. Confidence (parrēsia, παρρησία) is boldness. We come not timidly but confidently. God hears means He responds favorably. This is not carte blanche but submission. Asking according to His will means surrendering our desires to His purposes. The Spirit helps us pray this way (Romans 8:26-27). God hears and answers prayers aligned with His will.

If we know He hears us, we know we have what we asked. Assurance of being heard produces assurance of receiving. God does not merely listen; He grants. This assumes prayers are according to His will (v. 14). Faith trusts that what is asked in accordance with God's will is as good as done. The timing may vary, but the answer is certain. Confidence in prayer flows from confidence in God's character.

If you see a brother or sister commit a sin that does not lead to death, pray and God will give them life. Intercession for sinning believers. Not all sin leads to death. There is sin that leads to death, and John does not say to pray about that. This is difficult. Sin leading to death may refer to apostasy (Hebrews 6:4-6, 10:26-27), blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (Matthew 12:31-32), or physical death as judgment (Acts 5:1-11, 1 Corinthians 11:30). John distinguishes degrees of sin without specifying. Prayer for sinning believers is commanded and effective. But some sin places one beyond intercession. Sobering warning.

All wrongdoing is sin. Every deviation from God's standard is sin, not just egregious offenses. But there is sin that does not lead to death. Not all sin results in ultimate judgment. Distinction between sins unto death and sins not unto death. All sin is serious, but not all sin has the same consequence. Context suggests the unforgivable sin or apostasy as sin leading to death. Repentant sin is forgivable; unrepentant, persistent, blasphemous sin may not be.

Anyone born of God does not continue to sin. Echoes 3:6,9. Habitual sin is incompatible with new birth. The One who was born of God (Christ) keeps them safe. Jesus protects believers. The evil one cannot harm them. Satan cannot snatch believers from Christ's hand (John 10:28-29). Security is in Christ, not in self. Believers are guarded by divine power. Sin is not their master; Christ is their keeper. Assurance of protection.

We know we are children of God. Assurance repeated. The whole world is under the control of the evil one. Stark contrast. Two realms: God's family and the world under Satan. The world lies in the evil one's power (present tense). Satan is the god of this age (2 Corinthians 4:4), the prince of this world (John 12:31). Believers are rescued from his domain (Colossians 1:13). We are in the world but not of it. Spiritual warfare is the backdrop of Christian life.

The Son of God has come and has given us understanding. Christ's coming brings revelation. We may know Him who is true. Knowing God is the goal. We are in Him who is true by being in His Son Jesus Christ. Union with Christ is union with the Father. He is the true God and eternal life. Jesus is identified as God and life. This is one of Scripture's clearest affirmations of Christ's deity. Jesus is not a way to God; He is God. He is not a way to life; He is life. The letter ends with high Christology. Jesus is true God and eternal life.

Dear children, keep yourselves from idols. Abrupt, surprising ending. Idols are anything that replaces God. In context, false teaching about Christ is idolatry. Denying Jesus' deity or humanity creates a false god. But application is broader: anything loved more than God is an idol. Guard your hearts. Protect your worship. Do not substitute anything for the true God revealed in Jesus Christ. Final warning: avoid all counterfeits. Stay true to the gospel. Keep yourselves from idols. Amen.
